local_names.romanized_local: Saba Teishoku (Japanese); Saba Ikan Nasi Set (Malay romanized)

region_of_origin: Japan (dish origin); widely served in Singapore

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a specific entry for saba fish rice set as a composite dish; closest anchor is mackerel food product (FOODON_00002809). Rice component not separately anchored.
ontology.foodex2_description: Atlantic mackerel — used as closest FoodEx2 anchor for saba (Pacific mackerel) composite rice dish; no exact FoodEx2 code exists for the composite set meal.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a composite code for saba fish rice set. A0CKP (Atlantic mackerel) is the closest defensible anchor. Rice component is not separately coded here.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for saba fish rice set as a composite dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard saba fish rice set serving is approximately 350 g, comprising roughly 150 g of grilled saba mackerel fillet and 200 g of steamed white rice, as commonly portioned in Singapore hawker centres and Japanese casual restaurants.

nutrient_source.title: Saba Fish Rice Set — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Saba Fish Rice Set (cr_id F05100115). All seven reported nutrients (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre) are taken at face value from the HPB record with confidence 0.95. Extended micronutrient fields not reported by HPB are set to -1 per numeric hygiene rules.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for the composite saba fish rice set. The GI of 72 is assigned based on the dominant carbohydrate component: white rice (jasmine/short-grain type), which has a well-established GI of approximately 72 per the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283). The fish and fat components do not contribute meaningfully to GI. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (72 × 20.48 × 3.5) / 100 = 72 × 71.68 / 100 = 51.6 for a 350 g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 174 mg/100 g; composite meal sodium may be higher with added soy sauce or miso soup
hypertension.guidance: The base dish sodium of 174 mg/100 g is below the 200 mg/100 g threshold, and the omega-3 fatty acids in saba mackerel support endothelial function; however, patients with hypertension should request reduced soy sauce and low-sodium condiments, as the full set meal with sauces may exceed 400 mg sodium per serving.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: White rice GI ~72 (high); carbohydrate 20.48 g/100 g with only 1.72 g/100 g dietary fibre — insufficient to moderate postprandial glycaemic response
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with Type 2 Diabetes should substitute white rice with brown rice or cauliflower rice, reduce the rice portion to no more than half the standard serving, and increase the saba mackerel component, as the fish itself has a negligible glycaemic impact and provides beneficial omega-3 fatty acids.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saba mackerel is rich in EPA and DHA omega-3 fatty acids; saturated fat approximately 2.8 g/100 g is below the 5 g threshold; trans fat is 0 g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Grilled saba mackerel is among the most cardioprotective fish choices due to its high EPA/DHA content, which reduces triglycerides and supports anti-inflammatory pathways; patients with cardiovascular disease should consume this dish regularly with a reduced white rice portion and avoid deep-fried preparation or high-sodium sauces.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High EPA/DHA omega-3 content from saba mackerel directly reduces joint inflammation per EULAR dietary guidelines
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Saba mackerel is one of the strongest dietary interventions for rheumatoid arthritis due to its omega-3 content; patients should ensure grilled preparation to preserve EPA/DHA, limit soy sauce to reduce sodium-driven tissue swelling, and consider substituting white rice with brown rice to reduce the glycaemic-inflammatory load.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Saba Fish Rice Set is a popular Japanese-influenced set meal widely served in Singapore hawker centres, food courts, and Japanese casual dining restaurants. The dish consists of a grilled saba (Pacific mackerel, Scomber japonicus) fillet served alongside a portion of steamed white rice, typically accompanied by a small side salad, pickled vegetables, or miso soup depending on the establishment. The saba fillet is usually salted and grilled over direct heat or on a grill pan, producing a lightly charred, crispy skin with moist, flaky, richly flavoured flesh. Saba mackerel has a distinctively bold, savoury, slightly oily flavour profile that pairs well with the neutral starchiness of Japanese-style short-grain or jasmine white rice. Nutritionally, the dish provides 230 kcal per 100 g, with 10 g protein, 11.6 g fat, and 20.5 g carbohydrate. The fat profile is notable for its high omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acid content (EPA and DHA), making saba one of the most nutritionally significant fish for cardiovascular and anti-inflammatory health. Dietary fibre is modest at 1.72 g/100 g, primarily from the rice component. Sodium is 174 mg/100 g from the base dish, though this may increase substantially with added soy sauce or miso soup. The white rice component carries a high glycaemic index of approximately 72, making portion control important for individuals managing blood glucose. The dish is halal-compatible when prepared without alcohol-based marinades and is dairy-free. It is a staple lunch and dinner option across Singapore and is culturally associated with Japanese teishoku (定食) set meal traditions adapted for the local market.

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre) are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database with a reported confidence of 0.95 and provenance marked as canonical. The HPB is the primary approved source for Singapore food records. Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central mackerel reference (NDB 15046) as HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this composite dish. GI is an analogous estimate based on the white rice component from the University of Sydney GI Database. Extended micronutrients not reported by HPB are set to -1.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (2.8 g/100 g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central entry for cooked Atlantic mackerel (NDB 15046) and white rice composite, as HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this dish. This is an approximation and should be verified against a direct HPB or ICMR-NIN composite analysis if available. 2. Trans fat is reported as 0.0 g — confirmed zero for grilled fish and plain rice preparation; no hydrogenated fats are used in standard preparation. 3. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are set to -1: detailed micronutrient and fatty acid breakdown for this composite saba fish rice set is not separately reported in the HPB Foodo database as of 2026. USDA FoodData Central provides component-level data for mackerel and rice individually but not for this specific composite dish as served in Singapore. 4. GI of 72 is an analogous estimate based on the white rice dominant carbohydrate component per University of Sydney GI Database. No direct GI measurement for the composite saba fish rice set has been published. Glycemic load per 350 g serving = 72 × 71.68 / 100 = 51.6. 5. The input record had gi_value: 0 and gi_level: null from an 'Embedded GI Database' — this has been corrected to a defensible analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database as required by Project Veritas v2 source policy. 6. Halal status is conditional on preparation method — no alcohol-based marinades or non-halal ingredients should be used.
